title: About
text:
I’m a Senior Data Scientist based in San Francisco with 11+ years of progressive experience spanning operations, analytics, and data science.
Career path: Started in operations at ZocDoc, transitioned into analytics at Square (now Block), and grew into a senior data science role at Atlassian over 6 years. Also taught data analytics at General Assembly.
What I do: Build targeting models, experimentation frameworks, and AI-powered tools that help teams make better decisions. At Atlassian, my work attributed $20M in bookings and $50M in pipeline to enterprise marketing programs.
What sets me apart: My path through operations and teaching means I communicate analysis in terms stakeholders actually act on. I also led AI adoption for a 40+ person data org — training, tooling, and hands-on enablement with LLMs, RAG, and agentic workflows.
What I’m looking for: Senior data science roles where I can combine analytical rigor with AI-augmented workflows, ideally at companies pushing the boundaries of how data teams work.

title: Context Agent
text:
The Problem
Data teams answer the same business questions differently depending on who’s asking. A VP of Sales wants pipeline forecasts with revenue implications. A product manager wants user engagement metrics with cohort breakdowns. The underlying data is the same — the context is what changes.
Context Agent is an AI-powered data analyst that automatically adapts its analysis style, metric selection, and communication approach based on who it’s talking to.

title: Context Agent
text:
How It Works
The agent uses Claude’s tool use capabilities to:

Load stakeholder context — Markdown profiles defining each audience’s priorities, KPIs, and communication preferences
Query data — Execute SQL against a DuckDB database with synthetic GTM data
Adapt the analysis — Frame results, choose visualizations, and adjust technical depth based on the active stakeholder context


Architecture

Agent loop — Claude API with tool use for multi-step reasoning
Context system — Configurable markdown files (stakeholder profiles, domain knowledge, analysis frameworks)
Execution layer — Sandboxed SQL and Python execution via DuckDB
UI — Streamlit app for interactive exploration

title: Context Agent
text:
Technical Decisions

DuckDB over SQLite — Columnar storage is better suited for analytical queries. Supports window functions and CTEs natively.
Markdown for context — Easier to read, version, and customize than YAML or JSON. Stakeholder profiles read like documentation.
Tool use over RAG — The agent needs to act (run queries, generate charts), not just retrieve information. Tool use gives it the ability to reason and execute.

title: Context Agent
text:
Stack
Python | Claude API | DuckDB | Streamlit | Pandas | Plotly

title: Lead Scoring & Buying Group Identification
text:
The Problem
In B2B SaaS, marketing teams target thousands of accounts but only a fraction will close. The challenge is twofold:

Which accounts are most likely to buy? — Propensity scoring ranks accounts by conversion likelihood so marketing can allocate resources efficiently.
What’s preventing accounts from closing? — Buying group analysis identifies who’s missing at each account (roles, seniority, functions) and recommends specific enrichment actions.

This project builds both layers and combines them into a targeting framework that maps directly to how GTM teams operate.

title: Lead Scoring & Buying Group Identification
text:
Key Results

Propensity Model (Logistic Regression)



Metric
Value




Test AUC
0.608


Precision @ Top 10%
54.5% (vs. 36% baseline)


Top-Decile Lift
1.4x



The model uses 49 features across three layers — firmographic/technographic signals, contact composition metrics, and buying group characteristics. Top drivers include VP+ engagement on deals, complementary tech stack, and buying group completeness.


Buying Group Analysis



Completeness Tier
Win Rate
Accounts




Complete (75-100)
51%
146


High (50-75)
38%
176


Medium (25-50)
27%
170


Low (0-25)
24%
87



Accounts with complete buying groups win at 2x the rate of those with low completeness. The gap analysis identified 464 accounts with specific coverage gaps and estimated $286M in addressable pipeline from enrichment.

title: Lead Scoring & Buying Group Identification
text:
Methodology

Synthetic Data
Four tables mirror a real CRM stack: accounts (1,500 companies with firmographic and technographic data), contacts (10,504 people with job titles, functions, seniority), opportunities (800 deals), and contact_opportunity (1,944 role assignments linking people to deals).
Win probability is influenced by realistic signals: VP+ contacts on the deal, technical + business function mix, complementary tech stack, buying group size, segment, and industry.


Feature Engineering
Features are built at three levels, from weakest to strongest signal:

Firmographic/Technographic — segment, employee count, revenue, tech stack flags, existing customer status
Contact Composition — VP+ count, function diversity, senior density, technical + business mix
Buying Group — deal contact count, role coverage, completeness score (0-100)



Model Selection
Logistic regression was chosen over random forest despite marginal AUC improvement (0.608 vs 0.652) because:

Coefficients translate directly to scoring rules (“VP+ engagement adds X points”)
Mirrors what production marketing stacks (Marketo, HubSpot) actually use
Easier to explain to non-technical stakeholders
Regularization (L2, C=0.01) handles feature redundancy



Buying Group Framework
Each account’s buying group is scored on four dimensions (25 points each):

Role coverage — Champion, Decision Maker, Evaluator, Influencer
Seniority mix — VP+ and Director/Manager level contacts
Function diversity — contacts across 2+ departments
Technical + Business — both builders and buyers at the table

Gap accounts are paired with propensity scores to create a 2x2 targeting framework that prioritizes enrichment where it will have the highest ROI.

title: Lead Scoring & Buying Group Identification
section: What I’d Do Differently in Production
text:
What I’d Do Differently in Production

Title normalization — Real job titles are messy. Would use fuzzy matching or an LLM to standardize before feature extraction.
Temporal validation — Train on earlier time periods, test on later ones. Time-based splits are more realistic than random splits.
Live scoring pipeline — Integrate with Salesforce/HubSpot via scheduled batch scoring or event-driven updates.
Model monitoring — Track AUC and calibration over time. Retrain when performance degrades.
A/B test the model — Randomly assign accounts to model-scored vs. control groups and measure incremental lift.

title: Lead Scoring & Buying Group Identification
